column7:
Louise Bourgeois (1911 - 2010, France) was a French-American artist renowned for her innovative and deeply personal works that explore themes of memory and emotion. Born in Paris and later settling in New York, Bourgeois’s early experiences, particularly her complex family dynamics and her parents’ tapestry restoration business, were central influences on her art.

Bourgeois’s influence on contemporary art is profound, with her groundbreaking work challenging traditional forms and narratives. She was the first living woman sculptor to receive a retrospective at the Museum of Modern Art in New York in 1982. Her art continues to resonate for its raw emotional honesty and universal relevance.
column8: Louise Bourgeois’s “Maman” (1999) installed at Khao Yai Art Forest, Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand, 2024. © The Easton Foundation/VAGA at ARS, NY. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Nawaphon Phokrachang, Rungkit tungtongpol, Pongsakorn Sukreuangrong, Krittawat Atthsis, and Puttisin Choojesroom.
column9: Sculpture, Bronze, Stainless Steel, Marble, Motherhood, Memory, Nature, Regenerative Agriculture

column7:
Fujiko Nakaya (b. 1933, Japan) is a Japanese artist celebrated for her groundbreaking fog sculptures, which blend art, science, and nature. Born in Sapporo, Japan, she is the daughter of Ukichiro Nakaya, a renowned physicist who created the first artificial snowflakes. Inspired by her father’s scientific explorations, Nakaya developed her signature medium—water vapor—creating ephemeral sculptures that respond to environmental conditions such as wind and temperature.

Her work transcends traditional boundaries of sculpture, offering immersive, sensory experiences that invite viewers to engage with the natural world. Nakaya’s fog installations have been featured in major venues worldwide, including the Guggenheim Museum and the Tate Modern.
column8: Fujiko Nakaya, “Khao Yai Fog Forest”, Fog Landscape #48435, 2024. Courtesy of the artist and Khao Yai Art. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Nawaphon Phokrachang, Chittawan Limcharoen, Nut Fotofixed, and Ratsiree Rattanawan.
column9: Fog Sculpture, Land Art, Mixed Media, Pure Water, Environment, Science and Art, Immersive

column7:
Elmgreen & Dragset (b. 1961 & 1969, Denmark & Norway) is a Scandinavian artist duo consisting of Michael Elmgreen and Ingar Dragset. Collaborating since 1995, they are known for their conceptual and installation art, which blends humor, social critique, and poetic sensitivity. Their work often challenges conventions of public and private spaces, addressing issues of identity, power structures, and cultural norms.

Elmgreen & Dragset’s work has been exhibited in major museums and biennales globally, and they remain influential figures in contemporary art for their ability to create thought-provoking and immersive experiences that engage with societal issues in unexpected ways.
column8: Elmgreen & Dragset, “K-BAR”, 2024, mixed media. Courtesy of the artists and Khao Yai Art. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Chinnakit Ruenma, Rungkit Tangtongpon, Nut Fotofixed, Krittawat Atthsis, and Puttisin Choojesroom
column9: Mixed Media, Installation, Social Critique, Gastronomy, Martin Kippenberger, Land Art

column7:
Francesco Arena (b. 1978, Italy) is a contemporary artist whose practice explores the intersections of history, memory, and materiality. Working primarily with stone, metal, and wood, Arena develops sculptures and installations that translate political, social, and personal narratives into condensed material forms. His work is characterized by minimal gestures that carry significant conceptual and historical weight, often addressing themes of absence, time, and the limits of representation.

Arena has exhibited widely at international institutions, including the Venice Biennale and MAXXI – National Museum of 21st Century Arts, Rome. Through his sustained engagement with matter as a bearer of memory, Arena creates contemplative spaces in which form becomes a site of ethical and existential inquiry.
column8: Francesco Arena, “GOD”, 2024, stones. Courtesy of the artist and Khao Yai Art. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Chinnakit Ruenma, Chittawan Limcharoen, Krittawat Atthsis, and Puttisin Choojesroom.
column9: Stone Sculpture, Minimalist, Spirituality, Memory, Language, Site-Specific

column7:
ubatsat (pseudonym) (b. 1980, Thailand) holds a bachelor’s degree in Visual Arts and a master’s degree in Philosophy and Religion from Chiang Mai University. He is an artist and social activist whose research-based practice examines conditions of pressure, precarity, and crisis.

His work frequently develops through collaboration with communities—including farmers, artisans, musicians, cooks, and field specialists—using artistic platforms as spaces for collective exchange. His multidisciplinary practice spans painting, sculpture, printmaking, and site-specific installations.
column8: ubatsat, “Pilgrimage to Eternity”, 2024, mixed media. Courtesy of the artist and Khao Yai Art. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Nut Fotofixed, Krittawat Atthsis, and Puttisin Choojesroom.
column9: Site-specific Sculpture, Buddhist Philosophy, Stupa, Impermanence, Thai Artist, Land Art

column7: Scenocosme overturns various technologies in order to create interactive installations which evolve thanks to bodily and social relations of the audience. Their works came from possible hybridizations between the technology and living world, which meeting points incite them to invent sensitive and poetic languages.
column8: Scenocosme, “Pulsus Vitae”, Pulsations, 2013, sound installation. Scenocosme: Grégory Lasserre & Anaïs met den Ancxt. Courtesy of Domaine du Muy and the artists. Photo by Chittawan Limcharoen and Ratsiree Rattanawan.
column9: Sound Installation, Interactive, Technology, Nature, Heartbeat, Sensory

column7:
Araya Rasdjarmrearnsook (b. 1957, Thailand) is one of Thailand’s most influential contemporary artists. Working across video, installation, performance, and sculpture, her practice examines life, death, identity, and the limits of communication. Deeply informed by Thai cultural contexts and Buddhist philosophy, her work addresses universal human experiences through a localized and experiential lens.

Rasdjarmrearnsook has exhibited widely at major international platforms, including the Venice Biennale, Documenta, and leading institutions such as Tate Modern. She is a professor of fine arts and remains a central figure in Southeast Asian contemporary art, known for a practice that foregrounds listening, vulnerability, and the ethics of speaking to—and with—those who may never respond.
column8: Araya Rasdjarmrearnsook, “Two Planets Series”, (2007 - 2008), video installation. Courtesy of the artist and Khao Yai Art. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Krittawat Atthsis, Puttisin Choojesroom, and Chinnakit Ruenma.
column9: Video Installation, Thai Village, Western Art, Cultural Exchange, Buddhist Philosophy, Communication

column7:
Richard Long (b. 1945, UK) is a British artist internationally recognized as a pioneer of Land Art. Born in Bristol, England, Long’s practice revolves around walking as both a creative act and a means of engaging with the natural world. His works often document his journeys through landscapes, incorporating materials like stones, mud, and driftwood gathered along the way.

Long has exhibited extensively, including at the Tate Britain, the Museum of Modern Art, and Documenta. He is the recipient of numerous accolades, including the Turner Prize (1989). His minimalist yet profound interventions in nature continue to influence contemporary art, highlighting the harmonious relationship between humans and the natural world.
column8: Richard Long, “Madrid Circle”, 1988, stones. Courtesy of the artist and Khao Yai Art. Photo by Andrea Rossetti, Chittawan Limcharoen, Chinnakit Ruenma, and Nawaphon Phokrachang.
column9: Slate Stones, Land Art, Walking, Minimalist, Geometric, Temporal
